title: Poker Fundamentals
subtitle: Rules, streets, and how a hand unfolds
intro: Texas Hold'em is the most widely studied poker variant. Each player receives two private hole cards, then five community cards are dealt in stages: the flop (three cards), the turn (one card), and the river (one card).

Betting happens before the flop (preflop), after the flop, after the turn, and after the river. You win by making the best five-card hand from your hole cards plus the board, or by being the last player remaining after everyone else folds.
Understanding the flow of a hand — who acts when, what the blinds are, and when cards are revealed — is the foundation for every strategic concept that follows.

Every hand you play sits inside a larger framework: Game Theory Optimal (GTO) strategies aim to be unexploitable by mixing correct frequencies, while exploitative play deviates when opponents leak chips through predictable folds, calls, or sizings. You do not need solver software to benefit — simply knowing that each street has a pot size, a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR), and an action order lets you ask the right question: am I getting the right price to continue, and does my position let me control the pot? Fundamentals are not just rules; they are the scaffolding for every pot-odds calculation and range decision that follows.

primer.heading: How a hand unfolds
body: Texas Hold'em deals each player two private **hole cards**, then reveals five shared **community cards** in stages. You make your best five-card hand from any combination of your two cards and the board.
Betting happens on four **streets**. Knowing the order cold is the foundation for everything else.

keyTerms.definition: Betting right after hole cards are dealt; no board yet.
First 3 community cards, followed by a betting round.
The 4th and 5th community cards, each with its own betting round.
If callers remain after the river, hands are revealed and the best one wins.

visual.caption: The four betting streets, in order

primer.heading: Blinds and stakes
body: Two forced bets seed the pot each hand: the **small blind** (left of the dealer) and the **big blind** (one more seat left). A game is named by these two numbers.

In a `$1/$2` game the small blind posts `$1` and the big blind posts `$2`. Pros measure stacks in **big blinds** (bb), not dollars, so strategy transfers across stakes: a `$200` stack at `$1/$2` is `100bb`.

keyTerms.definition: Forced bet left of the dealer — the first number in the stakes, e.g. `$1`.
Forced bet one seat further, the second number, e.g. `$2`. Also the unit for measuring stacks.

primer.heading: Position: the button is power
body: The **dealer button** rotates each hand. Whoever has it acts **last** on every postflop street, seeing what everyone else does before deciding.

Acting last is a permanent information edge: you control the size of the pot and bluff or value-bet with more certainty. 'In position' (IP) beats 'out of position' (OOP) — play more hands on the button, fewer from the blinds.

visual.title: Why position wins money
visual.text: Postflop action starts with the first player left of the button and ends **on** the button. Last to act = most information = best decisions.

primer.heading: Stack-to-pot ratio (SPR)
body: **SPR** = effective stack ÷ pot size at the start of a street. It tells you how much room there is to maneuver — and how committed you are.
**High SPR** (say 10+) means deep play with many streets to come; implied odds and position matter. **Low SPR** (1–3) means you're close to committed — decisions collapse toward 'all-in or fold'.

visual.caption: Effective stack = the smaller of the two stacks that can still be wagered.

example.prompt: `$1/$2`, effective stacks `$200`, three players see a flop with `$18` in the pot. What is the SPR?
steps: Divide the effective stack by the pot.
example.result: SPR ≈ 11 — deep, so plan multiple streets rather than committing now.

primer.heading: Pot odds: the price to continue
body: **Pot odds** turn a call into a math problem: compare the chips you must put in to the total you can win. The breakeven equity is `call / (call + pot you'd win)`.
If your chance of making the best hand beats that breakeven number, calling is profitable in the long run. This single comparison underlies almost every drawing decision in poker.

visual.caption: Call when your hand's equity exceeds this percentage.

example.prompt: Villain bets `$10` into a `$20` pot, so there is `$30` to win. You must call `$10`. What equity do you need?
steps: You risk `$10` to win the `$30` already out there.
example.result: You need ≥ 25% equity to call profitably (the pot lays you 3-to-1).

primerNarration.title: How a hand unfolds
primerNarration.caption: Texas Hold'em gives each player two private hole cards. Then five community cards arrive in stages — the flop, the turn, and the river — with a round of betting on each street. If callers remain after the river, it goes to showdown and the best five-card hand wins.

primerNarration.title: Forced bets seed the pot
primerNarration.caption: Two forced bets start every hand. The small blind sits left of the dealer; the big blind is one seat further. A one-two game means a small blind of one and a big blind of two. The big blind is also the unit pros use to measure stacks.
bullets: Stacks are counted in big blinds, not dollars

primerNarration.title: One-two stakes, 100 big blinds
primerNarration.caption: Naming the stakes tells you the blinds. In one-two, a two hundred dollar stack is one hundred big blinds. Measuring in big blinds lets the same strategy work whether you play small or high stakes.

primerNarration.title: The button acts last
primerNarration.caption: The dealer button rotates each hand, and whoever holds it acts last after the flop. Acting last means you see everyone else's decisions first — a permanent information edge. Play more hands in position and fewer from the blinds.

visual.text: Last to act = most information = best decisions.

primerNarration.title: SPR sizes the decision
primerNarration.caption: Stack-to-pot ratio is the effective stack divided by the pot. A high ratio, around ten or more, means deep play with many streets to come. A low ratio, one to three, means you are nearly committed. Two hundred in stack over an eighteen-dollar pot is about eleven.

primerNarration.title: Are you getting the right price?
primerNarration.caption: Pot odds compare the chips you must call to the total you can win. The breakeven equity is your call divided by the pot plus your call. Calling ten to win thirty means ten over forty, or twenty-five percent. If your hand wins more often than that, calling profits.

questions.question: How many private hole cards does each player receive in Texas Hold'em?

explanations.A: Hold'em uses one private card only in exotic variants; standard Texas Hold'em always deals two.
explanations.B: Correct. Every player gets exactly two hole cards that only they may look at until showdown.
explanations.C: Four hole cards is Omaha, not Hold'em.
explanations.D: Five private cards would leave no community cards for the shared board.

remediation.question: At the start of a Hold'em hand, each player is dealt this many face-down cards:

explanations.B: Three is not standard Hold'em.
explanations.C: Five is the community board total, not hole cards.
explanations.D: Seven is used in some stud games, not Hold'em.

remediation.question: Your hidden cards in Texas Hold'em are called hole cards. How many do you get?

explanations.B: Correct — exactly two hole cards.
explanations.D: Six isn't a standard deal in Hold'em.

questions.question: How many community cards are dealt on the board in a full Texas Hold'em hand?

explanations.A: Three is only the flop; the turn and river add two more.
explanations.B: Four is still missing the river.
explanations.C: Correct — flop (3) + turn (1) + river (1) = 5 community cards.
explanations.D: Seven would exceed a standard deck's shared cards.

remediation.question: The flop shows three community cards. After the turn and river, the board has:

explanations.A: Four is only through the turn.
explanations.B: Correct — five total community cards.

remediation.question: In Hold'em you combine your hole cards with community cards. The board ends with:

explanations.B: Four is missing the river.

questions.question: What is the correct order of betting rounds in Hold'em?

explanations.B: Correct — preflop, then flop, turn, and river.
explanations.D: River always comes after the turn.

remediation.question: After hole cards are dealt, the first betting round is:

explanations.B: Correct — betting starts preflop.

remediation.question: The turn is dealt after which street?

explanations.A: Preflop has no community cards yet.
explanations.B: Correct — flop, then turn.

questions.question: In a $1/$2 cash game, the small blind posts:

explanations.A: Correct — the first number is the small blind.
explanations.B: The big blind is $2.

remediation.question: The player directly left of the dealer posts the small blind of:
options: Half the big blind only in tournaments

explanations.B: That's the big blind.
explanations.C: Cash games use fixed blinds as labeled.

remediation.question: In $1/$2, the big blind is:

explanations.B: Correct — the second number is the big blind.

questions.question: At showdown, a player must show cards to win the pot if:
options: They were the last aggressor on the river
They are called and must table a hand to claim the pot

explanations.A: Last aggressor rule decides show order, not eligibility.
explanations.B: Correct — if called at showdown you must show to win.
explanations.D: Checking preflop doesn't determine showdown rules.

remediation.question: If you bet the river and get called, you generally must:

explanations.A: You can't win without showing when called.
explanations.B: Correct — table your cards to claim the pot.
explanations.C: You didn't fold; you were called.
explanations.D: Full hand is shown at showdown.

remediation.question: A player who folded before the river:
options: Can still win if they had the nuts

explanations.A: Folded means out of the hand.
explanations.B: Correct — once you fold you cannot win the pot.
explanations.C: No need to show after folding.

questions.question: You hold A♠ K♦ and the board is Q♠ J♠ T♥ 2♣ 7♦. Your best five-card hand is:

explanations.A: You use A-K with the board to make Broadway: A-K-Q-J-T.
explanations.B: Correct — you have a Broadway straight (A high).
explanations.C: No five spades; not a flush.
explanations.D: Two pair isn't your best five here.

remediation.question: Hole cards A-K on board Q-J-T-x-x makes:

explanations.B: Correct — A-K-Q-J-T is a straight.

remediation.question: You may use zero, one, or two hole cards to make your best hand. Here A-K with Q-J-T uses:

explanations.A: Board alone is Q-J-T high card.
explanations.B: One card can't beat the straight.
explanations.C: Correct — both A and K complete the straight.
explanations.D: You choose the best five, not always both.

questions.question: Which action keeps you in the hand without putting more chips in (when no bet faces you)?

explanations.B: Correct — check passes action with no additional chips.

remediation.question: When no one has bet this street, you may:

explanations.B: Fold is allowed but not required.

remediation.question: Facing no bet, checking means:
options: You stay in without adding chips

explanations.B: Correct — pass action, stay in.

questions.question: Three players remain. Short stack goes all-in for $50, two deeper stacks build a $200 side pot. Who can win the side pot?

explanations.A: Short stack wasn't eligible for chips beyond their $50.
explanations.B: Correct — only players who matched the extra action contest the side pot.
explanations.C: Short stack can't win chips they didn't cover.

remediation.question: Side pots exist when:
options: A player is all-in for less than others continue betting

explanations.B: Correct — unequal all-ins create side pots.

remediation.question: The main pot is contested by:
options: Every player who matched the shortest all-in

explanations.A: All matched players can win main.
explanations.B: Correct — all who put in up to the short stack amount.
explanations.D: Main pot always has eligible players.

questions.question: Why is the dealer button considered valuable?
options: It posts the big blind next hand
It acts last on postflop streets

explanations.A: Button moves each hand; blind posting isn't the advantage.
explanations.B: Correct — acting last postflop lets you see others' actions first.

remediation.question: Postflop, the button typically acts:

explanations.B: Correct — last to act is the advantage.

remediation.question: Acting last on the flop lets you:

explanations.B: Dealer deals but that's not the strategic edge.

questions.question: Player A shoves all-in for $100. Player B has $300 and calls. Player C has $500 and raises to $250 total. What happens?
options: B may only call $100 unless they have chips to match C

explanations.A: Raises are legal if C has chips.
explanations.B: Correct — B must match available action; side pots may form if B can't cover full raise.

remediation.question: When a short all-in doesn't reopen betting, a full raise:
options: Always reopens action for players who already acted
May not reopen if the raise increment is insufficient

explanations.A: Reopening depends on raise size rules.
explanations.B: Correct — insufficient raises may not reopen.

remediation.question: Multiple all-ins at different stack depths usually create:

explanations.B: Correct — main pot plus side pot(s).
explanations.D: Split only at showdown if tied.

questions.question: $1/$2 cash game. Effective stacks are $200. Three players see a flop; the pot is $18 after preflop action. SPR (stack-to-pot ratio) is roughly:

explanations.A: Correct — $200 effective / $18 pot ≈ 11. High SPR means more postflop streets and implied odds matter.
explanations.B: ~2 would mean a much larger pot relative to stacks (e.g. $100 pot on $200 stacks).
explanations.C: ~100 would require a tiny pot, not $18.
explanations.D: ~0.5 would mean the pot is twice the remaining stack — a committed spot.

remediation.question: SPR = effective stack ÷ pot. Stack $150, pot $30. SPR is:

explanations.B: 0.2 inverts the ratio.

remediation.question: Low SPR (near 1–3) usually means:
options: Players are more committed — fewer streets to maneuver

explanations.A: Correct — small SPR often leads to all-in decisions quickly.
explanations.B: Low SPR limits room for future bets.
explanations.D: Postflop still matters, just with less stack behind.

questions.question: Six-max: UTG opens, MP calls, BTN calls, SB folds, BB checks. On the flop, who acts first?

explanations.A: Correct — postflop action starts with the first active player left of the button: the BB.
explanations.B: BTN acts last among remaining players, not first.
explanations.C: UTG is early but BB is left of button and acts first postflop.
explanations.D: MP acts after BB on the flop.

remediation.question: Postflop, the first player to act is:
options: First active seat left of the button

explanations.A: Correct — action starts left of button (often BB or SB if still in).
explanations.B: Button acts last postflop.
explanations.C: UTG is not always first postflop.

remediation.question: Preflop, the first player to act (excluding forced blinds) is typically:

explanations.A: Correct — UTG acts first preflop in standard order.
explanations.B: Button acts last preflop.
explanations.C: BB acts last preflop when unopened.

placementQuestions.question: Each Hold'em player receives how many hole cards?

explanations.B: Correct.

placementQuestions.question: Total community cards on the board:

explanations.B: Four is through turn.
Correct.
That's the big blind.

placementQuestions.question: To win when called on the river you must:

explanations.B: Correct.
explanations.D: Check doesn't claim a bet pot alone.

placementQuestions.question: Best hand uses at most how many hole cards?

explanations.B: One is common.

placementQuestions.question: No bet facing you — you can:

explanations.B: Fold optional.
Correct.
